---
title: "Covid 19 data Report"
author: "Sophia Syed"
date: "2026-03-02"
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE)
```

## R Markdown

This is an R Markdown document. Markdown is a simple formatting syntax for authoring HTML, PDF, and MS Word documents. For more details on using R Markdown see <http://rmarkdown.rstudio.com>.

When you click the **Knit** button a document will be generated that includes both content as well as the output of any embedded R code chunks within the document. You can embed an R code chunk like this:

#Introduction: This assignment takes data from the John Hopkins Covid Time Series Datasets (confirmed cases, reported deaths, and recovered cases). This assignment will be looking at various novelty trends (covid cases upon cruise liners) as well as Top 10 and Bottom 10 covid cases throughout the world, daily new covid cases for certain top 10 countries, case fatality rate for various top 10 countries, and a linear model to predict whether death is correlative with being diagnosed (US only)

Step 1/Chart 1: Read in the data from the raw github website then clean it of any white space or alternative characters. Rename some of the cities with their full names and make sure that there are no lags in the data (which is very likely since data tends to be dumped periodically if daily data is not temporarily available). I chose to look at the two cruise liners that were included in my dataset, since although their population is much less than a country (especially during social distancing and covid 19), their findings will be novel since sick individuals may have to cohabit in close quarters. Sadly, when one looks at the chart, one can see that both liners, especially the Diamond Princess, had spikes in early 2020, then suddenly plateaued to almost 0 for the rest of the covid period. This may be rather biased due to the fact that a lot of cruise lines halted service for many of their ships during the beginning of covid, as social restrictions grew strict. However, this is strange, since many cruiselines operated with reduced services after late 2020, signifying either high bias in the data or a large amount of missing data for these cruise liner variables. 
```{r}
library(tidyverse)
library(lubridate) 
library(janitor)

confirmed_cases <- "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/CSSEGISandData/COVID-19/master/csse_covid_19_data/csse_covid_19_time_series/time_series_covid19_confirmed_global.csv" 
deaths_cases <- "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/CSSEGISandData/COVID-19/master/csse_covid_19_data/csse_covid_19_time_series/time_series_covid19_deaths_global.csv" 
recovered_cases <- "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/CSSEGISandData/COVID-19/master/csse_covid_19_data/csse_covid_19_time_series/time_series_covid19_recovered_global.csv" 

total_confirmed <- read_csv(confirmed_cases) 
total_deaths <- read_csv(deaths_cases) 
total_recovered <- read_csv(recovered_cases)

confirmed_long <- total_confirmed %>% 
  pivot_longer(cols = matches("^\\d"), names_to = "date", values_to = "confirmed") %>% 
  mutate(date = mdy(date)) 

deaths_long <- total_deaths %>% 
  pivot_longer(cols = matches("^\\d"), names_to = "date", values_to = "deaths") %>%
  mutate(date = mdy(date)) 

recovered_long <- total_recovered %>% 
  pivot_longer(cols = matches("^\\d"), names_to = "date", values_to = "recovered") %>% 
  mutate(date = mdy(date))

covid_raw <- confirmed_long %>% 
  left_join(deaths_long, by = c("Province/State", "Country/Region", "Lat", "Long", "date")) %>%  left_join(recovered_long, by = c("Province/State", "Country/Region", "Lat", "Long", "date")) %>% 
  clean_names()


covid_clean <- covid_raw %>% 
  mutate( country_region = str_trim(country_region), 
          country_region = case_when( country_region %in% c("US", "United States of America", "USA") ~ "United States", 
                                      country_region == "Korea, South" ~ "South Korea", 
                                      country_region == "Taiwan*" ~ "Taiwan", 
                                      country_region == "Congo (Kinshasa)" ~ "Democratic Republic of Congo", country_region == "Congo (Brazzaville)" ~ "Republic of Congo", 
                                      country_region == "Burma" ~ "Myanmar", 
                                      country_region == "Czechia" ~ "Czech Republic", 
                                      country_region == "Holy See" ~ "Vatican City", 
                                      country_region == "West Bank and Gaza" ~ "Jeruselem", 
                                      TRUE ~ country_region ) )

covid_clean <- covid_clean %>% 
  group_by(country_region, province_state) %>% 
  arrange(date) %>%
  mutate( new_confirmed = confirmed - lag(confirmed, default = 0), 
          new_deaths = deaths - lag(deaths, default = 0), 
          new_recovered = recovered - lag(recovered, default = 0) ) %>% ungroup()

covid_clean <- covid_clean %>% 
  mutate( geo_type = case_when( country_region %in% c("Diamond Princess", "MS Zaandam") ~ "cruise_liner", is.na(province_state) | province_state == "" ~ "country", 
TRUE ~ "province" ) )

covid_ships <- covid_clean %>% 
  filter(geo_type == "cruise_liner") 

ggplot(covid_ships, aes(x = date, y = new_confirmed, color = country_region)) + 
  geom_line(linewidth = 0.5) +
  geom_point(size = 1, aes(shape = country_region)) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c( "Diamond Princess" = "#D81B60", "MS Zaandam" = "#1E88E5" )) + 
  labs( title = "Daily New COVID‑19 Cases on Cruise Ships", x = "Date", y = "New Cases", color = "Ship", shape = "Ship" ) + 
  theme_classic(base_size = 10)
```
Chart 2: I decided to look at the top 15 countries, during 2023 (end of the reporting period), to see the amount of total reported cases. This data again would be rather biased, since USA, India and some European countries have more advanced public health departments and resources and are more likely to consistently report findings, compared to their other counterparts. I am rather shocked that China is not in this list, since it is one of the worlds largest countries, and is believed to be the original spot of the disease. 
```{r}
latest_date <- max(covid_clean$date, na.rm = TRUE) 

top15 <- covid_clean %>% 
  filter(date == latest_date, geo_type == "country") %>% 
  group_by(country_region) %>% 
  summarize(total_confirmed = sum(confirmed, na.rm = TRUE)) %>% 
  arrange(desc(total_confirmed)) %>% 
  slice_head(n = 15) 

  ggplot(top15, aes(x = reorder(country_region, total_confirmed), y = total_confirmed)) + 
  geom_col(fill = "beige") + 
  #coord_flip() + 
  labs( title = "Top 15 Countries by Total Confirmed Cases", x = "Country", y = "Total Cases" ) + 
  theme_minimal(base_size = 7)
```
Chart 3: Looking at the Bottom 15 countries/areas by number of accumulated confirmed cases in March 2023. I included the Summer/ Winter Olympics within this chart since it was
interesting that a 16 day event would have almost as many cases as the continent of Antarctica or Vatican City. I also decided that this data is quite biased since many of the countries in the chart are third world countries/areas and do not have the same amount of public health resources that other areas (e.g. USA, France and India) would to help give a more realistic count of total cases. 
```{r}
latest_date <- max(covid_clean$date, na.rm = TRUE) 

bottom_15count <- covid_clean %>% 
  filter(date == latest_date, geo_type == "country") %>% 
  group_by(country_region) %>%
  summarize(total_confirmed = sum(confirmed, na.rm = TRUE)) %>% 
  arrange(total_confirmed) %>% 
  slice_head(n = 15)

ggplot(bottom_15count, aes(x = reorder(country_region, total_confirmed), 
                     y = total_confirmed)) + 
  geom_col(fill = "#528B8B") + 
  coord_flip() +
  labs( title = "Bottom 15 Countries/Areas by Total Confirmed Cases", 
        subtitle = latest_date, x = "Country/Area", y = "Total Confirmed Cases" ) + theme_classic(base_size = 12)
```
Chart 4: Measure the top 12 countries where fatalities per total cases triumphs (where the country count is not 0). I decided to play around with the data in a somewhat biased way by checking the top 12 countries where fatalities occur, which may be skewed due to certain countries purposefully reporting more cases than others, and having better public health departments with more specific/reliable data. I was surprised at the skewed data for North Korea, which was over 600% (a true outlier), while the others were all under 100%, many of them being third world/underdeveloped countries, which was no surprise due to the lack of quality preventative healthcare there. 
```{r}
cfr_top12 <- covid_clean %>% 
  filter(date == latest_date, geo_type == "country") %>%
  group_by(country_region) %>% 
  summarize( confirmed = sum(confirmed, na.rm = TRUE), 
             total_deaths = sum(deaths, na.rm = TRUE), 
             fatal = total_deaths / confirmed ) %>% 
             filter(confirmed > 0) %>% 
             arrange(desc(fatal)) %>% 
  slice_head(n = 12)

ggplot(cfr_top12, aes(x = reorder(country_region, fatal), y = fatal)) + geom_col(fill = "#CD69C9") + scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::percent_format(accuracy = 0.50)) + labs( title = "Case Fatality Ratio (Deaths / Total Cases)", x = "Country", y = "CFR (%)" ) + scale_x_discrete(expand = expansion(mult = c(0.05, 0.1))) + theme_classic(base_size = 4) + theme( axis.text.x = element_text(margin = margin(t = 12)), axis.title.x = element_text(margin = margin(t = 16)), plot.margin = margin(18, 20, 18, 20) )
```

Chart 5: I wanted to play around with some of the top 6 countries based on daily new covid19 cases. I decided to do a time series chart to go with the time series data for the countries and noticed that only China had a minor peak in the beginning of 2020, while the European countries and US had multiple modes or peaks of daily reports throughout 2020-2023. Though France had a lot of negative daily reports, this may be due to biased data or lags in reporting for certain periods, but I included it to show the bias in the dataset.However, US had the most amount of daily cases and the largest amount of peaks or major modes due to the abundant amount of reported cases (all states and cities).

```{r}
covid_clean %>% 
  filter(country_region %in% c("Germany", "Brazil", "France", "India", "China", "USA", "US", "United States")) %>% 
  ggplot(aes(x = date, y = new_confirmed, color = country_region)) + 
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.2) + 
  scale_color_manual( values = c( "Brazil" = "#1f78b4", "France" = "#e31a1c", "India" = "#33a02c", "China" = "#ff7f00", "Germany" = "#CD950C", "United States" = "#FFC0CB"  ) ) + 
  labs( title = "Daily New COVID19 Cases - Top 6 Countries: France, India, China, USA, Germany, Brazil", x = "Year", y = "New Cases", color = "Country" ) + 
  theme_minimal(base_size = 14) + 
  theme( plot.title = element_text(face = "italic", size = 8, hjust = 0.25),
         axis.title = element_text(face = "bold.italic"), 
         axis.line = element_line(color = "grey"), 
         legend.position = "left", legend.title = element_text(face = "bold.italic") )
  
```
Chart 6 is a linear model just for USA and the number of deaths during Covid. I wanted to focus on the country with the largest amount of cases and possibly the least amount of reporting bias, due to more cases being noticed in USA. The data of deaths in USA shows a lot of exponential growth earlier on from Q1 to Q4, with a cumulative r2 factor of 0.975 showing a strong correlation between increasing cases in USA and a statistically significant p value. This model also has a slope of 1126, signifying that around 1126 deaths occurred each day throughout the reporting period. 

```{r}

lr_modelusa <- covid_clean %>% 
filter(country_region == "United States") %>% 
lm(deaths ~ date, data = .) 
summary(lr_modelusa)


```

Bias: Although this data is from the John Hopkins Website, there are many forms of bias in the data. Firstly, every country in the world (even those who belong to the WHO)will have a specific definition on what a "reported" covid case is, it may be covid-like symptoms or include false/positives or false negatives. We also do not know where this data was sampled from, maybe certain countries chose to derive their sources from hospital statistics while others (especially those from remote countries) just relied on estimates. One also does not know what constitutes a covid-recovery, as some individuals may not experience fatality but are left with other long term chronic illnesses and diseases after. Certain countries (e.g. Canada, China, France) are also looking at cases by province or state at times, while other countries are looking at the country as a whole, increasing the chance of skewed biased data. 

Conclusion: My initial predictions that the data would show various trends that more cases would be reported in Western countries, while third world countries would have the smallest numbers of reported cases but are more likely to have higher fatality rates from covid were true. The datasets used were solely longditudinal and did not take into consideration which neighbourhoods, the patients' SES, health conditions or environmental factors to make more sound judgements and conclusions about the data. I also predicted that the data would contain multiple modes especially during 2021 to 2022, when lockdown ended before the vaccines were distributed and during the second wave/variant of Covid. However, I was somewhat surprised by the cruise ship data since I expected it to be more volatile as it plateaued after early 2020s (outbreak stage), signifying a large amount of missing data, as travel was only suspended indefinitely during early-late lockdown time. 






You can also embed plots, for example:



Note that the `echo = FALSE` parameter was added to the code chunk to prevent printing of the R code that generated the plot.
